name: Anchoring
description: The tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information encountered when making decisions.
impact: Initial prices, numbers, or data points disproportionately skew all subsequent judgments and negotiations.
trigger_phrases: The original price was...
Starting from a baseline of...
Compared to the first estimate...
argument_patterns: First value X presented → all subsequent judgments adjusted relative to X
Initial anchor A → final estimate stays closer to A than evidence warrants
counter_questions: What would my estimate be if I had seen a different starting number?
Am I adjusting enough from the initial figure?
What independent evidence do I have for this value?
mitigation: Generate your own estimate before seeing external figures
Deliberately consider extreme values on both ends
Seek multiple independent data points

name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
description: Continuing a behavior or endeavor because of previously invested resources (time, money, effort) rather than future value.
impact: Leads to irrational persistence in failing projects or relationships, wasting additional resources.
trigger_phrases: We've already invested so much...
We can't quit now after all we've done
It would be a waste to stop
argument_patterns: Already invested X → must continue to justify X
Past cost C paid → present decision influenced by C rather than future outcomes
counter_questions: Would I start this project today if I hadn't already invested in it?
What advice would I give a friend in the same situation?
Am I continuing because of future value or past investment?
mitigation: Evaluate decisions based only on future costs and benefits
Ask 'what would a rational outsider advise?'
Reframe sunk costs as lessons rather than obligations

name: Availability Heuristic
description: Overestimating the likelihood of events that are easily recalled in memory, often because they are vivid, recent, or emotionally charged.
impact: Distorts risk assessment; causes overreaction to memorable but rare events while ignoring common but unmemorable ones.
trigger_phrases: I can think of several examples of...
This happens all the time
I just read about this recently
argument_patterns: Easily recalled examples of X → X must be common
Memorable event E → probability of E overestimated
counter_questions: Am I recalling examples easily because they're common, or because they're memorable?
What do the statistics actually say about this?
How recently or emotionally have I encountered this information?
mitigation: Seek statistical data rather than relying on recalled examples
Diversify information sources to avoid media-driven salience
Distinguish between vividness and frequency

name: Curse of Knowledge
description: Once you know something, it becomes very difficult to imagine not knowing it, leading to poor communication with those who lack that knowledge.
impact: Causes experts to communicate poorly with novices, creates teaching and leadership failures.
trigger_phrases: It's obvious that...
Everyone knows...
This is basic stuff
argument_patterns: Expert knows X → assumes audience also knows X → skips foundational explanation
Knowledge K acquired → expert forgets what it was like not to know K
counter_questions: Can I explain this assuming the listener has no prior knowledge?
What did I not understand before I learned this?
Am I assuming shared context that may not exist?
mitigation: Use analogies and concrete examples when teaching
Ask your audience what they already know before explaining
Seek feedback to check for gaps in understanding

name: Confirmation Bias
description: The tendency to search for, interpret, and remember information in a way that confirms one's existing beliefs.
impact: Reinforces incorrect beliefs, prevents updating of views with new evidence, creates echo chambers.
trigger_phrases: This proves what I've been saying
See, I was right all along
The evidence supports my view
argument_patterns: Belief B held → seek confirming evidence → ignore disconfirming evidence → B reinforced
New information I → interpreted to fit existing worldview W regardless of actual implications
counter_questions: What evidence would change my mind?
Am I giving equal weight to contradictory evidence?
Have I actively sought out opposing viewpoints?
mitigation: Actively seek disconfirming evidence
Engage with high-quality opposing arguments
Practice steel-manning the opposing view

name: Dunning-Kruger Effect
description: The tendency for people with limited knowledge in a domain to overestimate their own competence, while experts tend to underestimate theirs.
impact: Leads novices to make overconfident decisions; experts may fail to assert themselves adequately.
trigger_phrases: I've done a bit of reading and...
How hard can it be?
I think I understand this better than the experts
argument_patterns: Limited knowledge K → overestimated competence → poor decisions
Expert E with deep knowledge → aware of own ignorance → underconfident communication
counter_questions: How much do I actually know about this domain?
Am I aware of what I don't know?
What would an acknowledged expert say about my level of understanding?
mitigation: Seek feedback from true experts
Map out what you don't yet know before forming conclusions
Maintain intellectual humility and openness to correction

name: Belief Bias
description: Evaluating the strength of an argument based on the believability of its conclusion rather than the logical validity of its structure.
impact: Causes acceptance of logically invalid arguments that match existing beliefs and rejection of valid arguments that contradict them.
trigger_phrases: That conclusion sounds right to me
I believe the conclusion, so the reasoning must be sound
That can't be right because I don't believe the conclusion
argument_patterns: Conclusion C is believable → argument for C accepted regardless of logical validity
Conclusion C is unbelievable → argument for C rejected regardless of logical validity
counter_questions: Is the argument logically valid, independent of whether I believe the conclusion?
Would I accept this argument if it led to an opposite conclusion?
Am I evaluating the reasoning or just the outcome?
mitigation: Evaluate argument structure separately from conclusion plausibility
Practice formal logic exercises
Ask others to evaluate your reasoning without knowing your conclusion

name: Self-Serving Bias
description: The tendency to attribute positive outcomes to one's own character and negative outcomes to external factors.
impact: Prevents learning from failures and creates interpersonal conflict by shifting blame unfairly.
trigger_phrases: I succeeded because I worked hard
I failed because of bad luck / other people
The system is rigged against me
argument_patterns: Success S achieved → attributed to personal skill/virtue
Failure F occurred → attributed to external circumstances/others
counter_questions: What role did external factors play in my success?
What role did I play in this failure?
Would I explain someone else's identical outcome the same way?
mitigation: Conduct honest post-mortems after both successes and failures
Seek candid feedback from trusted advisors
Apply the same attribution standards to yourself as to others

name: Backfire Effect
description: When a person's core beliefs are challenged by contradictory evidence, they sometimes hold those beliefs even more strongly.
impact: Makes rational persuasion with facts counterproductive, entrenches misinformation.
trigger_phrases: I don't care what the evidence says
That just proves they're trying to deceive us
The more they deny it, the more I believe it
argument_patterns: Belief B challenged with evidence E → B strengthened rather than weakened
Counterargument perceived as attack on identity → defensive reinforcement of original belief
counter_questions: What evidence would genuinely change my mind on this?
Am I treating this as an attack on me personally rather than on an idea?
Can I separate my identity from this belief?
mitigation: Separate identity from beliefs
Engage with contrary views in a safe, low-stakes context
Focus on process-based persuasion rather than fact-dumping

name: Barnum Effect
description: The tendency to accept vague, general statements as uniquely applicable to oneself.
impact: Makes people susceptible to pseudoscience, horoscopes, cold readings, and manipulative flattery.
trigger_phrases: You are a person who sometimes...
At times you feel...
People see you as...
argument_patterns: Vague statement V applies to almost everyone → person P accepts V as personally specific
Flattering generality F presented → received as unique insight into P
counter_questions: Would this statement apply equally to most people?
How specific and falsifiable is this claim about me?
Am I flattered into acceptance?
mitigation: Ask whether the statement is specific enough to be falsifiable
Test whether the description applies equally to others
Be skeptical of sources that seem to 'know you' without real information

name: Groupthink
description: The tendency for group cohesion and desire for conformity to override realistic appraisal of alternatives.
impact: Leads to poor collective decisions, suppresses dissent, and creates false consensus in teams and organizations.
trigger_phrases: We all agree on this, right?
Let's not rock the boat
Everyone on the team is on board
argument_patterns: Group consensus C desired → dissenting view D suppressed → poor decision reinforced
Social pressure P to conform → rational alternatives not considered
counter_questions: Have we genuinely considered alternatives?
Is anyone holding back their true opinion?
What would a devil's advocate say?
mitigation: Assign a formal devil's advocate role
Collect opinions anonymously before group discussion
Encourage structured dissent and pre-mortem analysis

name: Negativity Bias
description: The tendency to give more weight to negative experiences or information than positive ones of equal magnitude.
impact: Distorts risk perception, harms relationships and well-being, leads to overly cautious or fearful decisions.
trigger_phrases: But what about all the times it went wrong?
One mistake erases all the good work
I can't stop thinking about that criticism
argument_patterns: One negative event N outweighs many positive events P in memory and judgment
Potential loss L weighted more heavily than equivalent potential gain G
counter_questions: Am I giving equal weight to positive and negative evidence?
Would I make a different decision if I only focused on potential gains?
How often do the feared negative outcomes actually occur?
mitigation: Use pro-and-con lists to force balanced evaluation
Actively practice gratitude and cataloging of positive events
Use probabilistic thinking to assess realistic outcomes

name: Declinism
description: The tendency to view the past as better than it was and the future as worse than it is likely to be.
impact: Leads to poor long-term planning, resistance to change, and susceptibility to 'golden age' narratives.
trigger_phrases: Things were better back then
Society is going downhill
The world is getting worse every day
argument_patterns: Idealized past P vs. flawed present → resist progress
Selective memory M of good past events → present appears comparatively worse
counter_questions: What objective metrics can I use to compare past and present?
Am I remembering the past selectively?
What concrete improvements have occurred over time?
mitigation: Consult long-term data on measurable outcomes (life expectancy, poverty, literacy)
Distinguish between nostalgia and factual historical comparison
Seek out balanced historical accounts rather than idealized narratives

name: Framing Effect
description: The tendency to draw different conclusions from the same information depending on how it is presented.
impact: Makes individuals susceptible to manipulation through word choice, context, and presentation order.
trigger_phrases: 90% survival rate vs. 10% mortality rate
You'll save $10 vs. you'll lose $10
Half full vs. half empty
argument_patterns: Same fact F presented positively → different decision than F presented negatively
Loss framing L triggers more caution than equivalent gain framing G
counter_questions: How would I react if this were presented differently?
Am I responding to the frame or the underlying facts?
Can I restate this in a neutral way?
mitigation: Reframe information multiple ways before deciding
Focus on underlying data rather than the presentation
Be aware of how advertisers and politicians use framing deliberately

name: Fundamental Attribution Error
description: The tendency to attribute other people's actions to their character while attributing your own actions to situational factors.
impact: Creates unfair judgments of others and prevents empathy and understanding in social situations.
trigger_phrases: They did that because they're just that kind of person
She's always like that
He must be lazy / rude / incompetent
argument_patterns: Person P does action A → A attributed to P's character, ignoring situational context
Self does action A → A attributed to circumstances, not character
counter_questions: What situational factors might explain their behavior?
Would I behave differently in exactly the same situation?
Am I applying the same standards to them as to myself?
mitigation: Consider situational explanations before forming character judgments
Ask about the context before concluding
Practice perspective-taking and empathy

name: Halo Effect
description: The tendency for a positive impression in one area to positively influence opinions in unrelated areas.
impact: Causes biased hiring, performance reviews, and purchasing decisions based on irrelevant attractive qualities.
trigger_phrases: She's so impressive in X, she must be great at Y too
He seems trustworthy, so his business idea must be sound
They're attractive, so they must be competent
argument_patterns: Positive trait T in domain D1 → assumed positive trait in unrelated domain D2
First positive impression I → subsequent judgments biased positively regardless of evidence
counter_questions: Am I evaluating this specific trait on its own merits?
Would I judge this person the same if I disliked them overall?
What evidence do I have for this particular quality?
mitigation: Evaluate traits and decisions in isolation using structured criteria
Use blind evaluation processes where possible
Actively seek evidence that contradicts your overall impression

name: Optimism Bias
description: The tendency to overestimate the likelihood of positive outcomes and underestimate the likelihood of negative outcomes.
impact: Leads to underestimation of risks, poor planning for downside scenarios, and overconfident decision-making.
trigger_phrases: I'm sure it'll work out fine
That won't happen to me
I'll be the exception
argument_patterns: Future event E possible → own probability of E underestimated if E is negative
Own chances of success overestimated relative to base rates
counter_questions: What does the base rate suggest about outcomes in similar situations?
Am I accounting adequately for potential downsides?
What would a pessimist say about this plan?
mitigation: Use base rates and reference class forecasting
Conduct pre-mortem analysis (imagine failure and work backwards)
Balance optimism with scenario planning for negative outcomes

name: Pessimism Bias
description: The tendency to overestimate the likelihood of negative outcomes and underestimate the likelihood of positive ones.
impact: Leads to excessive risk aversion, missed opportunities, anxiety, and self-fulfilling negative outcomes.
trigger_phrases: Something will go wrong
There's no point in trying
It'll never work for me
argument_patterns: Future event E possible → own probability of E overestimated if E is negative
Success scenario underweighted → missed opportunities
counter_questions: What is the actual statistical probability of a negative outcome?
Am I avoiding opportunities due to imagined downsides?
Have I considered the costs of inaction?
mitigation: Track predictions and compare to outcomes to calibrate pessimism
Consider the cost of excessive caution alongside the cost of risk
Use cognitive-behavioral techniques to challenge catastrophic thinking

name: Just-World Hypothesis
description: The assumption that the world is fundamentally fair and people get what they deserve.
impact: Leads to victim blaming, reduced empathy, and complacency in the face of systemic injustice.
trigger_phrases: They must have done something to deserve it
Good things happen to good people
If they worked harder, they'd be better off
argument_patterns: Negative outcome O happened to person P → P must have deserved O
World W perceived as fair → systemic causes of inequality ignored
counter_questions: What structural or situational factors contributed to this outcome?
Can I think of cases where clearly good people experienced bad outcomes?
Am I blaming the victim to avoid confronting an unfair system?
mitigation: Seek structural and contextual explanations alongside personal ones
Learn about systemic inequalities and random variance in outcomes
Practice perspective-taking with those who face different circumstances

name: In-Group Bias
description: The tendency to favor members of one's own group over members of other groups.
impact: Creates unfair advantages for in-group members in hiring, trust, and resource allocation while disadvantaging out-groups.
trigger_phrases: We take care of our own
People like us understand
They're not one of us
argument_patterns: Person P belongs to same group G → P given benefit of the doubt
Person Q belongs to out-group → Q subject to more scrutiny and less trust
counter_questions: Would I judge this person the same if they were not in my group?
Am I giving credit based on group membership rather than merit?
What would I think of this behavior if an out-group member did it?
mitigation: Use blind evaluation processes for hiring and awards
Actively build relationships across group boundaries
Apply consistent standards regardless of group membership

name: Placebo Effect
description: The tendency for inert treatments to produce real, measurable effects due to the power of expectation and belief.
impact: Can lead to acceptance of ineffective treatments and make evaluation of interventions unreliable without controls.
trigger_phrases: I took it and felt better immediately
It worked for me, so it must work
Natural remedies always help
argument_patterns: Inert treatment T administered → real improvement I experienced → T credited with causing I
Expectation of improvement E → actual physiological improvement regardless of mechanism
counter_questions: Is this improvement due to the treatment or to my expectation?
Has this treatment been tested against a proper placebo control?
Would I have improved anyway without any treatment?
mitigation: Require randomized controlled trials before accepting medical claims
Be skeptical of testimonial-based evidence
Distinguish between subjective experience and objective measured outcomes

name: Bystander Effect
description: The tendency for individuals to be less likely to offer help in an emergency when others are present.
impact: Reduces emergency response rates, diffuses moral responsibility, and can lead to harm through inaction.
trigger_phrases: Someone else will handle it
There are plenty of other people here
Why should I be the one to step in?
argument_patterns: Emergency E occurs with crowd C present → each individual assumes another will act → no one acts
Diffusion of responsibility: many observers → individual responsibility diluted
counter_questions: If I don't act, who will?
Am I assuming someone else has already handled this?
Would I act if I were the only one present?
mitigation: Overcome diffusion by making a specific request to a specific person
Recognize the bystander effect and consciously override it
Train in emergency response to build default action habits

name: Reactance
description: The urge to do the opposite of what someone is trying to make you do when you feel your freedom is being restricted.
impact: Makes people resist beneficial advice, rules, or decisions purely because they feel coerced, even when the advice is correct.
trigger_phrases: You have to do this
You're not allowed to...
You must comply
argument_patterns: Directive D perceived as freedom constraint → opposite behavior O adopted
Persuasion attempt P perceived as manipulation → resistance R increased
counter_questions: Am I resisting because of rational objection or just because I feel controlled?
Would I make this choice freely if no one was pushing me?
What is my genuine preference when I remove the pressure?
mitigation: Present choices rather than directives
Provide autonomy and rationale when persuading
Recognize when your resistance is driven by reactance rather than reason

name: Spotlight Effect
description: The tendency to overestimate how much others notice and pay attention to one's appearance and behavior.
impact: Causes excessive self-consciousness, social anxiety, and inhibits authentic self-expression.
trigger_phrases: Everyone noticed that mistake I made
They're all looking at me
I can't let anyone see me like this
argument_patterns: Own appearance/action A salient to self → A assumed equally salient to others
Others' attention overestimated → excessive self-monitoring and anxiety
counter_questions: What percentage of people actually noticed this?
How much attention do I give to others' minor mistakes?
Would this matter to me in a week?
mitigation: Recognize that others are similarly preoccupied with their own concerns
Focus on how you make others feel rather than how you appear
Conduct reality checks by asking trusted friends
